verses.transliteration: kai eipen autois deute hymeis autoi kat idian eis erēmon topon kai anapauesthе oligon ēsan gar hoi erchomenoi kai hoi hypagontes polloi kai oude phagein ēukairoun
verses.study_note: 'Come ye yourselves apart' (deute hymeis autoi — you yourselves come away). 'Into a desert place' (eis erēmon topon — to a solitary, uninhabited area). 'Rest a while' (anapauesthе oligon — refresh yourselves a little). 'They had no leisure so much as to eat' — the same situation as 3:20. Jesus recognises his disciples' need for rest and renewal. Ministry without retreat leads to burnout. Jesus models the rhythm of engagement and withdrawal.

verses.kjv_text: And they departed into a desert place by ship privately.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπῆλθον εἰς ἔρημον τόπον τῷ πλοίῳ κατ᾿ ἰδίαν
verses.transliteration: kai apēlthon eis erēmon topon tō ploiō kat idian
verses.study_note: 'By ship privately' — they cross the lake by boat to find solitude. But the plan for rest will be interrupted by the crowd's need (v.33), and Jesus will respond with compassion rather than frustration.

verses.kjv_text: And the people saw them departing, and many knew him, and ran afoot thither out of all cities, and outwent them, and came together unto him.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶδον αὐτοὺς ὑπάγοντας καὶ ἐπέγνωσαν αὐτὸν πολλοί καὶ πεζῇ ἀπὸ πασῶν τῶν πόλεων συνέδραμον ἐκεῖ καὶ προῆλθον αὐτούς καὶ συνῆλθον πρὸς αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: kai eidon autous hypagontas kai epegnōsan auton polloi kai pezē apo pasōn tōn poleōn synedramon ekei kai proēlthon autous kai synēlthon pros auton
verses.study_note: 'Saw them departing' — the crowd watches the boat's direction. 'Ran afoot' (pezē synedramon — on foot they ran together). 'Outwent them' (proēlthon autous — arrived before them). The crowd runs around the northern shore of the lake and arrives at the destination before the boat. Their desperation to reach Jesus overrides all other concerns.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus, when he came out, saw much people, and was moved with compassion toward them, because they were as sheep not having a shepherd: and he began to teach them many things.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐξελθὼν εἶδεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς πολὺν ὄχλον καὶ ἐσπλαγχνίσθη ἐπ᾿ αὐτοῖς ὅτι ἦσαν ὡς πρόβατα μὴ ἔχοντα ποιμένα καὶ ἤρξατο διδάσκειν αὐτοὺς πολλά
verses.transliteration: kai exelthōn eiden ho Iēsous polyn ochlon kai esplanchnisthē ep autois hoti ēsan hōs probata mē echonta poimena kai ērxato didaskein autous polla
verses.study_note: 'Moved with compassion' (esplanchnisthē — deeply moved in his viscera; see 1:41). 'As sheep not having a shepherd' — echoing Numbers 27:17 and Ezekiel 34:5, where God condemns Israel's leaders for failing to shepherd his flock. Jesus sees not a crowd but a flock — leaderless, vulnerable, needing direction. 'He began to teach them' — the response to compassion is teaching. In Mark, the shepherd's first act is not feeding (that comes later, vv.35-44) but teaching.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is theologically rich. The image of 'sheep without a shepherd' echoes God's promise in Ezekiel 34:23: 'I will set up one shepherd over them, and he shall feed them.' Jesus fulfils this prophecy: he is the Good Shepherd promised by God. His compassion (splanchnizomai) is not mere emotion but divine motivation — it moves him to act. Significantly, his first response is to teach, not to heal or feed. The deepest human need is not physical but spiritual: people need the word of God before they need the bread of earth.

verses.kjv_text: And when the day was now far spent, his disciples came unto him, and said, This is a desert place, and now the time is far passed:
verses.original_text: καὶ ἤδη ὥρας πολλῆς γενομένης προσελθόντες αὐτῷ οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ λέγουσιν ὅτι ἔρημός ἐστιν ὁ τόπος καὶ ἤδη ὥρα πολλή
verses.transliteration: kai ēdē hōras pollēs genomenēs proselthontes autō hoi mathētai autou legousin hoti erēmos estin ho topos kai ēdē hōra pollē
verses.study_note: 'Day was far spent' (hōras pollēs — many hours having passed; late afternoon). 'Desert place' — the disciples state the practical problem: remote location, late hour, large crowd, no food. Their solution (v.36) is logical: dismiss the people to find food. Jesus' solution will be supernatural.

verses.kjv_text: Send them away, that they may go into the country round about, and into the villages, and buy themselves bread: for they have nothing to eat.
verses.original_text: ἀπόλυσον αὐτούς ἵνα ἀπελθόντες εἰς τοὺς κύκλῳ ἀγροὺς καὶ κώμας ἀγοράσωσιν ἑαυτοῖς ἄρτους τί γὰρ φάγωσιν οὐκ ἔχουσιν
verses.transliteration: apolyson autous hina apelthontes eis tous kyklō agrous kai kōmas agorasōsin heautois artous ti gar phagōsin ouk echousin
verses.study_note: 'Send them away' (apolyson — dismiss them). 'Buy themselves bread' — the disciples assume the crowd must fend for itself. Their suggestion is reasonable but unimaginative. Jesus is about to teach them that when God is present, human resources are not the limiting factor.

verses.kjv_text: He answered and said unto them, Give ye them to eat. And they say unto him, Shall we go and buy two hundred pennyworth of bread, and give them to eat?
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἀποκριθεὶς εἶπεν αὐτοῖς δότε αὐτοῖς ὑμεῖς φαγεῖν καὶ λέγουσιν αὐτῷ ἀπελθόντες ἀγοράσωμεν δηναρίων διακοσίων ἄρτους καὶ δῶμεν αὐτοῖς φαγεῖν
verses.transliteration: ho de apokritheis eipen autois dote autois hymeis phagein kai legousin autō apelthontes agorasōmen dēnariōn diakosiōn artous kai dōmen autois phagein
verses.study_note: 'Give ye them to eat' (dote autois hymeis phagein — you yourselves give them food; emphatic: you do it). 'Two hundred pennyworth' (dēnariōn diakosiōn — 200 denarii; about 8 months' wages for a labourer). The disciples calculate the impossibility: they cannot possibly buy enough bread. But Jesus has not asked them to buy; he has asked them to give. The difference between the two is the gap where miracles happen.

verses.kjv_text: He saith unto them, How many loaves have ye? go and see. And when they knew, they say, Five, and two fishes.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ λέγει αὐτοῖς πόσους ἄρτους ἔχετε ὑπάγετε καὶ ἴδετε καὶ γνόντες λέγουσιν πέντε καὶ δύο ἰχθύας
verses.transliteration: ho de legei autois posous artous echete hypagete kai idete kai gnontes legousin pente kai duo ichthyas
verses.study_note: 'How many loaves have ye?' — Jesus does not begin with the deficit but with the available resources. 'Go and see' — take inventory. 'Five, and two fishes' — five barley loaves (John 6:9) and two small fish. An absurdly small amount for thousands of people. But Jesus works with what is available. He does not need much; he needs what we have, offered to him.

verses.kjv_text: And he commanded them to make all sit down by companies upon the green grass.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπέταξεν αὐτοῖς ἀνακλῖναι πάντας συμπόσια συμπόσια ἐπὶ τῷ χλωρῷ χόρτῳ
verses.transliteration: kai epetaxen autois anaklinai pantas symposia symposia epi tō chlōrō chortō
verses.study_note: 'Commanded' (epetaxen — ordered). 'By companies' (symposia symposia — group by group; a Semitic idiom for orderly arrangement: dinner parties). 'Green grass' (chlōrō chortō — green vegetation; a detail indicating spring, near Passover, John 6:4). The organisation is deliberate: Jesus turns a chaotic mob into an ordered assembly, a wilderness into a banquet hall.

verses.kjv_text: And they sat down in ranks, by hundreds, and by fifties.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀνέπεσον πρασιαὶ πρασιαί ἀνὰ ἑκατὸν καὶ ἀνὰ πεντήκοντα
verses.transliteration: kai anepesan prasiai prasiai ana hekaton kai ana pentēkonta
verses.study_note: 'In ranks' (prasiai prasiai — row by row; literally 'garden beds,' referring to the orderly rectangular groups). 'By hundreds and by fifties' — the arrangement echoes Moses' organisation of Israel in the wilderness (Exodus 18:25). Jesus is the new Moses organising God's people for a wilderness feast. The scene deliberately evokes the Exodus: a shepherd-leader, a wilderness, an ordered people, and miraculous bread.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had taken the five loaves and the two fishes, he looked up to heaven, and blessed, and brake the loaves, and gave them to his disciples to set before them; and the two fishes divided he among them all.
verses.original_text: καὶ λαβὼν τοὺς πέντε ἄρτους καὶ τοὺς δύο ἰχθύας ἀναβλέψας εἰς τὸν οὐρανὸν εὐλόγησεν καὶ κατέκλασεν τοὺς ἄρτους καὶ ἐδίδου τοῖς μαθηταῖς αὐτοῦ ἵνα παραθῶσιν αὐτοῖς καὶ τοὺς δύο ἰχθύας ἐμέρισεν πᾶσιν
verses.transliteration: kai labōn tous pente artous kai tous duo ichthyas anablepsas eis ton ouranon eulogēsen kai kateklasen tous artous kai edidou tois mathētais autou hina parathōsin autois kai tous duo ichthyas emerisen pasin
verses.study_note: 'Looked up to heaven' (anablepsas eis ton ouranon — lifted his eyes heavenward; the posture of prayer). 'Blessed' (eulogēsen — gave thanks, spoke a blessing). 'Brake the loaves' (kateklasen — broke apart; the action of the host at a Jewish meal). 'Gave them to his disciples' (edidou — was giving; imperfect: he kept giving). 'Divided among them all' — Jesus took, blessed, broke, and gave — the four actions that will recur at the Last Supper (14:22). The disciples serve as intermediaries: Jesus provides, they distribute. The multiplication happens in Jesus' hands.

verses.kjv_text: And they did all eat, and were filled.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἔφαγον πάντες καὶ ἐχορτάσθησαν
verses.transliteration: kai ephagon pantes kai echortasthēsan
verses.study_note: 'All eat' (ephagon pantes — everyone ate). 'Were filled' (echortasthēsan — were satisfied, fully fed; from chortazō, to feed to the full, originally used of fattening cattle). Not a meagre portion but a satisfying meal. The miracle provides abundantly — enough for all, with surplus (v.43). The God who fed Israel with manna in the wilderness now feeds a new Israel through his Son.

verses.kjv_text: And they took up twelve baskets full of the fragments, and of the fishes.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἦραν κλασμάτων δώδεκα κοφίνους πλήρεις καὶ ἀπὸ τῶν ἰχθύων
verses.transliteration: kai ēran klasmatōn dōdeka kophinous plēreis kai apo tōn ichthyōn
verses.study_note: 'Twelve baskets' (dōdeka kophinous — twelve wicker baskets; the kophinos was a distinctly Jewish carrying basket). 'Full of fragments' (klasmatōn plēreis — full of broken pieces). Twelve baskets — one for each apostle who had said 'send them away.' The surplus is greater than the original supply. Jesus' provision is not merely sufficient but superabundant. The number twelve may also symbolise provision for all twelve tribes of Israel.

verses.kjv_text: And they that did eat of the loaves were about five thousand men.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἦσαν οἱ φαγόντες τοὺς ἄρτους πεντακισχίλιοι ἄνδρες
verses.transliteration: kai ēsan hoi phagontes tous artous pentakischilioi andres
verses.study_note: 'Five thousand men' (pentakischilioi andres — five thousand males). Matthew 14:21 adds 'beside women and children,' suggesting the total crowd may have been 15,000-20,000. This is the only miracle (besides the resurrection) recorded in all four Gospels, emphasising its centrality. Five loaves fed five thousand — a thousand-fold multiplication.

verses.kjv_text: And straightway he constrained his disciples to get into the ship, and to go to the other side before unto Bethsaida, while he sent away the people.
verses.original_text: καὶ εὐθέως ἠνάγκασεν τοὺς μαθητὰς αὐτοῦ ἐμβῆναι εἰς τὸ πλοῖον καὶ προάγειν εἰς τὸ πέραν πρὸς Βηθσαϊδάν ἕως αὐτὸς ἀπολύσῃ τὸν ὄχλον
verses.transliteration: kai eutheōs ēnagkasen tous mathētas autou embēnai eis to ploion kai proagein eis to peran pros Bēthsaidan heōs autos apolysē ton ochlon
verses.study_note: 'Constrained' (ēnagkasen — compelled, forced). 'Get into the ship' — Jesus urgently sends the disciples ahead. John 6:15 explains why: the crowd wanted to make Jesus king by force. Jesus disperses both the crowd and the disciples to prevent a political uprising. 'While he sent away the people' — Jesus handles the volatile crowd alone.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had sent them away, he departed into a mountain to pray.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποταξάμενος αὐτοῖς ἀπῆλθεν εἰς τὸ ὄρος προσεύξασθαι
verses.transliteration: kai apotaxamenos autois apēlthen eis to oros proseuxasthai
verses.study_note: 'Departed into a mountain to pray' — the second major prayer scene in Mark (cf. 1:35). After the intensity of the feeding miracle and the temptation of political kingship, Jesus withdraws to pray. The mountain is a place of divine encounter (cf. Moses, Elijah). Prayer is Jesus' response to both success and temptation.

verses.kjv_text: And when even was come, the ship was in the midst of the sea, and he alone on the land.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὀψίας γενομένης ἦν τὸ πλοῖον ἐν μέσῳ τῆς θαλάσσης καὶ αὐτὸς μόνος ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς
verses.transliteration: kai opsias genomenēs ēn to ploion en mesō tēs thalassēs kai autos monos epi tēs gēs
verses.study_note: 'Even was come' (opsias genomenēs — evening arrived). 'In the midst of the sea' — alone on the water. 'He alone on the land' — a visual separation: the disciples struggling at sea, Jesus alone on the mountain. Yet he is watching them (v.48). The scene symbolises the church's experience: Jesus is physically absent, the disciples are struggling, but he sees them and will come to them.

verses.kjv_text: And he saw them toiling in rowing; for the wind was contrary unto them: and about the fourth watch of the night he cometh unto them, walking upon the sea, and would have passed by them.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδὼν αὐτοὺς βασανιζομένους ἐν τῷ ἐλαύνειν ἦν γὰρ ὁ ἄνεμος ἐναντίος αὐτοῖς καὶ περὶ τετάρτην φυλακὴν τῆς νυκτὸς ἔρχεται πρὸς αὐτοὺς περιπατῶν ἐπὶ τῆς θαλάσσης καὶ ἤθελεν παρελθεῖν αὐτούς
verses.transliteration: kai idōn autous basanizomenous en tō elaunein ēn gar ho anemos enantios autois kai peri tetartēn phylakēn tēs nyktos erchetai pros autous peripatōn epi tēs thalassēs kai ēthelen parelthein autous
verses.study_note: 'Saw them' (idōn — he saw; from the mountain, in the dark, across the water). 'Toiling' (basanizomenous — being tormented, distressed; the same word used for torture). 'Contrary wind' (enantios — opposing, hostile). 'Fourth watch' (tetartēn phylakēn — 3-6 AM; the Roman division of the night). 'Walking upon the sea' (peripatōn epi tēs thalassēs — walking on the surface of the water). 'Would have passed by them' (ēthelen parelthein — intended to pass by; echoing the divine 'passing by' in Exodus 33:19-22 and 1 Kings 19:11 — theophanies where God reveals himself).
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase 'would have passed by them' is one of the most theologically loaded in Mark. In the OT, God 'passes by' Moses (Exodus 33:19-22) and Elijah (1 Kings 19:11) as an act of self-revelation. Jesus is not trying to avoid the disciples; he is revealing himself as Yahweh walking on the waters of chaos (Job 9:8, Psalm 77:19). The sea-walking is a theophany — a divine self-disclosure. Jesus does on the Sea of Galilee what only God does in the Old Testament: treads upon the waves.

verses.kjv_text: But when they saw him walking upon the sea, they supposed it had been a spirit, and cried out:
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ ἰδόντες αὐτὸν περιπατοῦντα ἐπὶ τῆς θαλάσσης ἔδοξαν φάντασμα εἶναι καὶ ἀνέκραξαν
verses.transliteration: hoi de idontes auton peripatounta epi tēs thalassēs edoxan phantasma einai kai anekraxan
verses.study_note: 'Supposed it had been a spirit' (edoxan phantasma einai — they thought it was a ghost/apparition). 'Cried out' (anekraxan — screamed). The disciples, already exhausted from hours of futile rowing against the wind, see a figure walking on the water in the pre-dawn darkness and are terrified. Their fear is understandable — what they are seeing is humanly impossible.

verses.kjv_text: For they all saw him, and were troubled. And immediately he talked with them, and saith unto them, Be of good cheer: it is I; be not afraid.
verses.original_text: πάντες γὰρ αὐτὸν εἶδον καὶ ἐταράχθησαν καὶ εὐθέως ἐλάλησεν μετ᾿ αὐτῶν καὶ λέγει αὐτοῖς θαρσεῖτε ἐγώ εἰμι μὴ φοβεῖσθε
verses.transliteration: pantes gar auton eidon kai etarachthēsan kai eutheōs elalēsen met autōn kai legei autois tharseite egō eimi mē phobeisthe
verses.study_note: 'They all saw him' — not a private vision but a shared experience. 'Troubled' (etarachthēsan — agitated, terrified). 'Be of good cheer' (tharseite — take courage). 'It is I' (egō eimi — I AM). 'Be not afraid' (mē phobeisthe — stop being afraid). The words egō eimi echo the divine self-revelation of Exodus 3:14: 'I AM THAT I AM.' In the OT, 'I am, do not fear' is the characteristic speech of Yahweh appearing to his people (Isaiah 41:10, 43:1-3). Jesus identifies himself with the divine name.
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase egō eimi ('It is I' / 'I AM') is the theological climax of this passage. On one level, Jesus is simply identifying himself: 'It's me, don't be afraid.' On a deeper level, he is using the divine self-identification formula of Exodus 3:14 and Isaiah 43:1-3. Combined with walking on water (a divine prerogative, Job 9:8) and the 'passing by' motif (Exodus 33:19), this is a full theophany — God revealing himself to his people in their distress. The disciples do not yet understand (v.52), but Mark wants the reader to see: the one in the boat is Yahweh in the flesh.

verses.kjv_text: And he went up unto them into the ship; and the wind ceased: and they were sore amazed in themselves beyond measure, and wondered.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀνέβη πρὸς αὐτοὺς εἰς τὸ πλοῖον καὶ ἐκόπασεν ὁ ἄνεμος καὶ λίαν ἐκ περισσοῦ ἐν ἑαυτοῖς ἐξίσταντο καὶ ἐθαύμαζον
verses.transliteration: kai anebē pros autous eis to ploion kai ekopasen ho anemos kai lian ek perissou en heautois existanto kai ethaumazon
verses.study_note: 'Went up into the ship' — Jesus boards the boat. 'Wind ceased' (ekopasen — died down, became weary; as in 4:39). 'Sore amazed beyond measure' (lian ek perissou existanto — exceedingly, utterly out of themselves with amazement). The compound intensifiers (lian, ek perissou) indicate the most extreme astonishment recorded in Mark.

verses.kjv_text: For they considered not the miracle of the loaves: for their heart was hardened.
verses.original_text: οὐ γὰρ συνῆκαν ἐπὶ τοῖς ἄρτοις ἦν γὰρ ἡ καρδία αὐτῶν πεπωρωμένη
verses.transliteration: ou gar synēkan epi tois artois ēn gar hē kardia autōn pepōrōmenē
verses.study_note: 'Considered not' (ou synēkan — did not understand; from syniēmi, to put together). 'The miracle of the loaves' — if they had understood the feeding miracle (Jesus is the divine provider), they would not have been surprised by the sea-walking (Jesus is the divine Lord of nature). 'Their heart was hardened' (pepōrōmenē — petrified, calloused; perfect tense: a settled condition). Mark uses the same word (pōroō) for the Pharisees in 3:5 — a startling comparison. The disciples' spiritual perception is dangerously slow.
verses.doctrinal_note: This editorial comment by Mark is devastating. The disciples' amazement at the sea-walking reveals their failure to comprehend the feeding miracle. The two miracles are connected: the one who multiplied bread is the one who walks on water — both are acts of the Creator God. If they had understood who fed the 5,000, they would have known who was walking toward them. 'Their heart was hardened' uses the same word applied to Jesus' opponents. The disciples are not hostile like the Pharisees, but they are equally slow to perceive Jesus' true identity. Understanding comes not from seeing miracles but from perceiving who performs them.

verses.kjv_text: And when they had passed over, they came into the land of Gennesaret, and drew to the shore.
verses.original_text: καὶ διαπεράσαντες ἦλθον ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν Γεννησαρὲτ καὶ προσωρμίσθησαν
verses.transliteration: kai diaperasantes ēlthon epi tēn gēn Gennēsaret kai prosōrmisthēsan
verses.study_note: 'Gennesaret' — a fertile plain on the northwestern shore of the Sea of Galilee, south of Capernaum. 'Drew to the shore' (prosōrmisthēsan — moored, anchored). The storm is over, and the boat reaches land safely — as Jesus' word always guarantees arrival.

verses.kjv_text: And when they were come out of the ship, straightway they knew him,
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐξελθόντων αὐτῶν ἐκ τοῦ πλοίου εὐθέως ἐπιγνόντες αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: kai exelthontōn autōn ek tou ploiou eutheōs epignontes auton
verses.study_note: 'Straightway they knew him' (eutheōs epignontes — immediately recognising). The people of Gennesaret recognise Jesus instantly. His fame precedes him. Unlike Nazareth, where familiarity bred contempt, this region receives him eagerly.

verses.kjv_text: And ran through that whole region round about, and began to carry about in beds those that were sick, where they heard he was.
verses.original_text: περιδραμόντες ὅλην τὴν περίχωρον ἐκείνην ἤρξαντο ἐπὶ τοῖς κραβάττοις τοὺς κακῶς ἔχοντας περιφέρειν ὅπου ἤκουον ὅτι ἐκεῖ ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: peridramontes holēn tēn perichōron ekeinēn ērxanto epi tois krabattois tous kakōs echontas peripherein hopou ēkouon hoti ekei estin
verses.study_note: 'Ran through' (peridramontes — ran around, scoured the entire region). 'Carry about in beds' (peripherein epi krabattois — carry on pallets; portable mats). 'Where they heard he was' — the sick are brought to wherever Jesus happens to be. The region mobilises around Jesus' presence — a spontaneous mass movement of desperation and faith.

verses.kjv_text: And whithersoever he entered, into villages, or cities, or country, they laid the sick in the streets, and besought him that they might touch if it were but the border of his garment: and as many as touched him were made whole.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὅπου ἂν εἰσεπορεύετο εἰς κώμας ἢ πόλεις ἢ ἀγροὺς ἐν ταῖς ἀγοραῖς ἐτίθουν τοὺς ἀσθενοῦντας καὶ παρεκάλουν αὐτὸν ἵνα κἂν τοῦ κρασπέδου τοῦ ἱματίου αὐτοῦ ἅψωνται καὶ ὅσοι ἂν ἥπτοντο αὐτοῦ ἐσῴζοντο
verses.transliteration: kai hopou an eiseporeueto eis kōmas ē poleis ē agrous en tais agorais etithoun tous asthenountas kai parekaloun auton hina kan tou kraspedou tou himatiou autou hapsōntai kai hosoi an ēptonto autou esōzonto
verses.study_note: 'Villages, cities, or country' — everywhere without exception. 'In the streets' (en tais agorais — in the marketplaces). 'The border of his garment' (tou kraspedou tou himatiou — the fringe/tassel of his cloak; the tzitzit, the tassels commanded in Numbers 15:38-39). 'As many as touched him were made whole' (hosoi ēptonto esōzonto — all who touched were healed/saved; imperfect: this kept happening, repeatedly). The chapter ends with a summary of universal healing — a counterpoint to the Nazareth rejection. Where faith receives Jesus, his power flows without limit.

3.meaning: Carpenter, woodworker, craftsman, builder — from tiktō (to produce, create). A tektōn in first-century Galilee was a general construction worker who worked with wood, stone, and building materials. The term covers everything from furniture making to house construction. Jesus was known in Nazareth as a manual labourer — the village craftsman. This detail reveals that God incarnate spent most of his earthly life in humble, physical work. The Creator of the universe was known as 'the carpenter.' Mark alone calls Jesus himself a tektōn; Matthew 13:55 says 'carpenter's son.'

5.meaning: Power, mighty work, miracle — from dynamai (to be able). In v.5, the startling phrase 'he could do no mighty work' (oudemian dynamin poiēsai) does not mean Jesus lacked power but that his power operates within the context of faith. Miracles in Mark are not magical tricks performed for amazement but kingdom signs that require receptive hearts. The limitation is volitional, not ontological: Jesus chose not to force his power on the unwilling. God's dynamis is not diminished by unbelief, but its expression is self-limited by divine respect for human freedom.

7.meaning: To send forth, commission, dispatch on a mission — from apo (from) + stellō (to send). The noun form is apostolos (apostle, sent one). In v.7, Jesus 'began to send them forth' (apostellein) — the verbal root of their title. An apostle is not a volunteer but one commissioned by authority greater than himself. The sending implies both the authority of the sender and the obedience of the sent. The apostles go not in their own name or power but in Jesus', carrying his authority over unclean spirits and his message of repentance.

34.meaning: To be moved with compassion — from splanchna (viscera, inner organs). This visceral compassion drives Jesus to action: he sees shepherdless sheep and responds by teaching (v.34), then feeding (vv.41-42). In Mark, splanchnizomai always precedes a mighty act: healing a leper (1:41), teaching the crowds (6:34), feeding the 4,000 (8:2). Jesus' miracles are not demonstrations of power but responses of compassion. The divine heart feels before the divine hand acts. This word reveals the emotional interior of God incarnate.

41.meaning: To bless, speak well of, give thanks — from eu (well, good) + logos (word). Jesus 'blessed' the five loaves and two fishes — speaking God's goodness over the meagre provision. The blessing acknowledges God as the source of all provision and transforms the ordinary into the extraordinary. The same sequence (took, blessed, broke, gave) appears at the Last Supper (14:22), connecting the feeding miracle to the Eucharist. In both, Jesus takes what is insufficient, blesses it, and distributes abundance.

48.meaning: To walk, walk about, conduct one's life — from peri (around) + pateō (to tread, walk). The phrase 'walking on the sea' (peripatōn epi tēs thalassēs) echoes Job 9:8 LXX, where God alone 'walks on the waves of the sea.' What is God's exclusive prerogative in the OT becomes Jesus' action in the NT. The Sea of Galilee, which symbolises chaos and threat in Mark (4:37-39), is now beneath Jesus' feet. He does not merely calm the chaos; he treads upon it as sovereign Lord.

50.meaning: I am — the divine self-identification formula. In ordinary Greek, egō eimi means simply 'it is I' or 'I am he.' But in the context of a theophany (walking on water, passing by, calming storms), the phrase echoes Exodus 3:14 ('I AM THAT I AM') and the repeated 'I am he' of Isaiah (41:4, 43:10, 46:4). Jesus uses egō eimi at key moments in Mark: walking on water (6:50), before the Sanhedrin (14:62). Combined with the OT sea-walking imagery, this is a divine self-revelation: the one speaking is the God of Israel in human form.

52.meaning: To harden, petrify, make callous — from pōros (calcium deposit, stone). Applied to the disciples' hearts (pepōrōmenē, perfect passive: their hearts had been hardened and remained in that state). This is the same word used for the Pharisees (3:5) and for Israel (Romans 11:7). The disciples are not hostile but obtuse — they have seen extraordinary things but have not connected them to Jesus' identity. Hardness of heart in Mark is not intellectual inability but spiritual imperception: seeing without understanding, hearing without comprehending. It is the condition parables both diagnose and address (4:11-12).

meta.overview: Mark 7 marks a pivotal turning point in Jesus' ministry, shifting from controversy with Jewish leaders to outreach among Gentiles. The chapter opens with Pharisees and scribes from Jerusalem confronting Jesus because his disciples eat bread with unwashed ('defiled') hands (vv.1-5). Mark explains for his Gentile audience the Jewish tradition of ceremonial washing (vv.3-4). Jesus responds with devastating force, quoting Isaiah 29:13: 'This people honoureth me with their lips, but their heart is far from me' (v.6). He accuses them of 'laying aside the commandment of God' to hold their own traditions (v.8), and gives the specific example of Corban — dedicating money to the temple to avoid supporting one's parents, thus 'making the word of God of none effect' (vv.9-13). Jesus then teaches the crowd a revolutionary principle: 'There is nothing from without a man, that entering into him can defile him: but the things which come out of him, those are the things that defile the man' (v.15). Privately, he explains to the disciples that food enters the stomach and is expelled — 'purging all meats' (v.19), a parenthetical comment with enormous implications for the abolition of dietary laws. True defilement comes from within: 'evil thoughts, adulteries, fornications, murders' and a catalogue of sins that proceed from the human heart (vv.20-23). Jesus then withdraws into Gentile territory near Tyre, where a Syrophoenician woman begs him to cast a demon out of her daughter (vv.24-30). Her witty, faith-filled response to Jesus' test — 'Yes, Lord: yet the dogs under the table eat of the children's crumbs' — wins the miracle. The chapter closes in the Decapolis region, where Jesus heals a deaf man with a speech impediment using physical gestures (fingers in ears, spittle on tongue) and the Aramaic command 'Ephphatha' ('Be opened,' vv.31-37). The crowd's verdict: 'He hath done all things well: he maketh both the deaf to hear, and the dumb to speak' (v.37) — echoing Isaiah 35:5-6 and Genesis 1:31.
meta.theme: What Truly Defiles — Heart Over Ritual, and Grace Beyond Israel's Borders
meta.keyWordMeaning: Common, defiled, unclean — from koinoō (to make common or profane). In Jewish usage, koinos describes anything ritually impure through contact or failure to observe ceremonial washing. Jesus redefines defilement: it is not external contamination but internal corruption. What makes a person 'common' before God is not unwashed hands but an unwashed heart.
outline: Pharisaic Tradition vs. God's Commandment (vv.1-13): The Pharisees challenge Jesus over unwashed hands. He exposes their hypocrisy — honouring God with lips while their heart is far away. The Corban example shows how tradition can nullify God's word. Man-made rules substituted for divine commands

The Revolutionary Teaching on Defilement (vv.14-23): Nothing entering from outside defiles a person. Defilement comes from within the heart — evil thoughts adulteries fornications murders thefts covetousness wickedness deceit lasciviousness an evil eye blasphemy pride foolishness. All these evil things come from within and defile the man

The Syrophoenician Woman's Faith (vv.24-30): In Gentile territory near Tyre a Greek woman begs for her daughter's deliverance. Jesus tests her with the children-and-dogs metaphor. Her humble witty reply — the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs — demonstrates extraordinary faith and wins the miracle. The gospel crosses ethnic boundaries

Healing the Deaf and Mute in Decapolis (vv.31-37): Jesus heals a deaf man with a speech impediment using touch and the Aramaic Ephphatha (Be opened). The crowd declares He hath done all things well — echoing creation's verdict and fulfilling Isaiah 35:5-6. Messianic signs among Gentiles

verses.kjv_text: Then came together unto him the Pharisees, and certain of the scribes, which came from Jerusalem.
verses.original_text: καὶ συνάγονται πρὸς αὐτὸν οἱ Φαρισαῖοι καί τινες τῶν γραμματέων ἐλθόντες ἀπὸ Ἱεροσολύμων
verses.transliteration: kai synangontai pros auton hoi Pharisaioi kai tines tōn grammateōn elthontes apo Hierosolymōn
verses.study_note: The delegation is official — scribes 'from Jerusalem' indicates a formal investigation. The Pharisees and scribes form an alliance against Jesus, combining theological expertise with institutional authority. This is not a casual encounter but a calculated confrontation.

verses.kjv_text: And when they saw some of his disciples eat bread with defiled, that is to say, with unwashen, hands, they found fault.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδόντες τινὰς τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ κοιναῖς χερσίν τοῦτ᾿ ἔστιν ἀνίπτοις ἐσθίοντας ἄρτους ἐμέμψαντο
verses.transliteration: kai idontes tinas tōn mathētōn autou koinais chersin tout estin aniptois esthiontas artous emempsanto
verses.study_note: 'Defiled' (koinais — common, ritually unclean; not physically dirty but ceremonially impure). 'Unwashen' (aniptois — unwashed; Mark explains the technical Jewish term for his Gentile readers). 'Found fault' (emempsanto — blamed, censured). The issue is not hygiene but ritual purity.

verses.kjv_text: For the Pharisees, and all the Jews, except they wash their hands oft, eat not, holding the tradition of the elders.
verses.original_text: οἱ γὰρ Φαρισαῖοι καὶ πάντες οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι ἐὰν μὴ πυγμῇ νίψωνται τὰς χεῖρας οὐκ ἐσθίουσιν κρατοῦντες τὴν παράδοσιν τῶν πρεσβυτέρων
verses.transliteration: hoi gar Pharisaioi kai pantes hoi Ioudaioi ean mē pygmē nipsōntai tas cheiras ouk esthiousin kratountes tēn paradosin tōn presbyterōn
verses.study_note: 'Oft' (pygmē — literally 'with the fist,' meaning vigorously or up to the wrist; a specific washing technique). 'Tradition of the elders' (paradosin tōn presbyterōn — the oral law passed down from previous generations, later codified in the Mishnah). Mark explains Jewish customs for his Roman audience. These traditions were not in the Torah but were treated as equally binding.

verses.kjv_text: And when they come from the market, except they wash, they eat not. And many other things there be, which they have received to hold, as the washing of cups, and pots, brasen vessels, and of tables.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπὸ ἀγορᾶς ἐὰν μὴ βαπτίσωνται οὐκ ἐσθίουσιν καὶ ἄλλα πολλά ἐστιν ἃ παρέλαβον κρατεῖν βαπτισμοὺς ποτηρίων καὶ ξεστῶν καὶ χαλκίων καὶ κλινῶν
verses.transliteration: kai apo agoras ean mē baptisōntai ouk esthiousin kai alla polla estin ha parelabon kratein baptismous potēriōn kai xestōn kai chalkiōn kai klinōn
verses.study_note: 'Wash' (baptisōntai — immerse themselves; a full ritual immersion after visiting the marketplace where contact with Gentiles or unclean persons might have occurred). 'Washing' (baptismous — ritual immersions, ceremonial washings). The proliferation of ritual washings for cups, pots, bronze vessels, and even dining couches illustrates the elaborate purity system that had developed beyond Scripture.

verses.kjv_text: Then the Pharisees and scribes asked him, Why walk not thy disciples according to the tradition of the elders, but eat bread with unwashen hands?
verses.original_text: ἔπειτα ἐπερωτῶσιν αὐτὸν οἱ Φαρισαῖοι καὶ οἱ γραμματεῖς διὰ τί οὐ περιπατοῦσιν οἱ μαθηταί σου κατὰ τὴν παράδοσιν τῶν πρεσβυτέρων ἀλλὰ κοιναῖς χερσὶν ἐσθίουσιν τὸν ἄρτον
verses.transliteration: epeita eperōtōsin auton hoi Pharisaioi kai hoi grammateis dia ti ou peripatousin hoi mathētai sou kata tēn paradosin tōn presbyterōn alla koinais chersin esthiousin ton arton
verses.study_note: 'Walk' (peripatousin — conduct themselves, live). The Pharisees frame the question in terms of the disciples' lifestyle ('walk'), not a single incident. 'According to the tradition' — they appeal to the oral law's authority. The question assumes the tradition of the elders has binding authority equal to or greater than Scripture itself.

verses.kjv_text: He answered and said unto them, Well hath Esaias prophesied of you hypocrites, as it is written, This people honoureth me with their lips, but their heart is far from me.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἀποκριθεὶς εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ὅτι καλῶς ἐπροφήτευσεν Ἠσαΐας περὶ ὑμῶν τῶν ὑποκριτῶν ὡς γέγραπται οὗτος ὁ λαὸς τοῖς χείλεσίν με τιμᾷ ἡ δὲ καρδία αὐτῶν πόρρω ἀπέχει ἀπ᾿ ἐμοῦ
verses.transliteration: ho de apokritheis eipen autois hoti kalōs eprophēteusen Ēsaias peri hymōn tōn hypokritōn hōs gegraptai houtos ho laos tois cheilesin me tima hē de kardia autōn porrō apechei ap emou
verses.study_note: 'Hypocrites' (hypokritōn — actors, pretenders; originally used of stage actors who wore masks). Jesus quotes Isaiah 29:13 (LXX). 'Honoureth me with their lips' — external religious performance. 'Heart far from me' — internal spiritual distance. The devastating charge: their religion is performance, not relationship. This is the fundamental definition of hypocrisy: lips and heart in opposite directions.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus' use of Isaiah 29:13 establishes a permanent principle: God evaluates worship by the heart, not by outward conformity. Religious ritual without heart devotion is not merely inadequate but offensive — it is hypocrisy. This text became foundational for the Reformation's critique of externalism in religion and remains relevant wherever religious traditions substitute for genuine faith.

verses.kjv_text: Howbeit in vain do they worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men.
verses.original_text: μάτην δὲ σέβονταί με διδάσκοντες διδασκαλίας ἐντάλματα ἀνθρώπων
verses.transliteration: matēn de sebontai me didaskontes didaskalias entalmata anthrōpōn
verses.study_note: 'In vain' (matēn — to no purpose, futilely). 'Worship' (sebontai — revere, pay religious homage). 'Doctrines' (didaskalias — teachings, instructions). 'Commandments of men' (entalmata anthrōpōn — human precepts). Worship that teaches human rules as divine commands is vain — it achieves nothing before God. The most elaborate religious system is worthless if built on human authority rather than God's word.

verses.kjv_text: For laying aside the commandment of God, ye hold the tradition of men, as the washing of pots and cups: and many other such like things ye do.
verses.original_text: ἀφέντες γὰρ τὴν ἐντολὴν τοῦ θεοῦ κρατεῖτε τὴν παράδοσιν τῶν ἀνθρώπων βαπτισμοὺς ξεστῶν καὶ ποτηρίων καὶ ἄλλα παρόμοια τοιαῦτα πολλὰ ποιεῖτε
verses.transliteration: aphentes gar tēn entolēn tou theou krateite tēn paradosin tōn anthrōpōn baptismous xestōn kai potēriōn kai alla paromoia toiauta polla poieite
verses.study_note: 'Laying aside' (aphentes — abandoning, letting go). 'Hold' (krateite — grip tightly, hold fast). The tragic irony: they release God's command and grip human tradition. Jesus intensifies the charge — this is not mere addition to Scripture but active substitution. They have traded the divine for the human.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Full well ye reject the commandment of God, that ye may keep your own tradition.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἔλεγεν αὐτοῖς καλῶς ἀθετεῖτε τὴν ἐντολὴν τοῦ θεοῦ ἵνα τὴν παράδοσιν ὑμῶν τηρήσητε
verses.transliteration: kai elegen autois kalōs atheteite tēn entolēn tou theou hina tēn paradosin hymōn tērēsēte
verses.study_note: 'Full well' (kalōs — an ironic 'how nicely,' 'how expertly'). 'Reject' (atheteite — set aside, nullify, treat as invalid). 'Keep' (tērēsēte — observe, guard carefully). The sarcasm is biting: you are experts at annulling God's commandment in order to establish your own tradition. 'Your own' (hymōn) — Jesus will not even call it 'the tradition of the elders' anymore; it is their invention.

verses.kjv_text: For Moses said, Honour thy father and thy mother; and, Whoso curseth father or mother, let him die the death:
verses.original_text: Μωϋσῆς γὰρ εἶπεν τίμα τὸν πατέρα σου καὶ τὴν μητέρα σου καί ὁ κακολογῶν πατέρα ἢ μητέρα θανάτῳ τελευτάτω
verses.transliteration: Mōysēs gar eipen tima ton patera sou kai tēn mētera sou kai ho kakologōn patera ē mētera thanatō teleutatō
verses.study_note: Jesus appeals to Moses — the highest authority in Judaism — quoting the fifth commandment (Exodus 20:12) and its sanction (Exodus 21:17). 'Honour' (tima — value, prize, provide for; includes financial support). 'Curseth' (kakologōn — speaks evil of, reviles). The death penalty for cursing parents shows how seriously God views parental honour.

verses.kjv_text: But ye say, If a man shall say to his father or mother, It is Corban, that is to say, a gift, by whatsoever thou mightest be profited by me; he shall be free.
verses.original_text: ὑμεῖς δὲ λέγετε ἐὰν εἴπῃ ἄνθρωπος τῷ πατρὶ ἢ τῇ μητρί κορβᾶν ὅ ἐστιν δῶρον ὃ ἐὰν ἐξ ἐμοῦ ὠφεληθῇς
verses.transliteration: hymeis de legete ean eipē anthrōpos tō patri ē tē mētri korban ho estin dōron ho ean ex emou ōphelēthēs
verses.study_note: 'Corban' (korban — an offering, something dedicated to God; a Hebrew/Aramaic term Mark translates as dōron, 'gift'). By declaring possessions 'Corban,' a person could dedicate them to the temple while retaining personal use — effectively shielding assets from family obligations. The tradition created a legal loophole to avoid the fifth commandment.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Corban practice illustrates how religious systems can be manipulated to circumvent moral obligations. A son could pronounce his property 'Corban' (devoted to God) and thereby claim exemption from supporting his parents — even though the property remained in his own use. Jesus exposes this as using religion to avoid genuine duty. This principle applies whenever religious obligations are invoked to escape clear moral responsibilities.

verses.kjv_text: And ye suffer him no more to do ought for his father or his mother;
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐκέτι ἀφίετε αὐτὸν οὐδὲν ποιῆσαι τῷ πατρὶ αὐτοῦ ἢ τῇ μητρὶ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai ouketi aphiete auton ouden poiēsai tō patri autou ē tē mētri autou
verses.study_note: 'Suffer him no more' (ouketi aphiete — you no longer permit). The tradition was enforced: once the Corban vow was made, the rabbis did not allow the person to reverse it, even if he recognized his obligation to his parents. The tradition trapped people in their evasion of God's law.

verses.kjv_text: Making the word of God of none effect through your tradition, which ye have delivered: and many such like things do ye.
verses.original_text: ἀκυροῦντες τὸν λόγον τοῦ θεοῦ τῇ παραδόσει ὑμῶν ᾗ παρεδώκατε καὶ παρόμοια τοιαῦτα πολλὰ ποιεῖτε
verses.transliteration: akyrountes ton logon tou theou tē paradosei hymōn hē paredōkate kai paromoia toiauta polla poieite
verses.study_note: 'Making of none effect' (akyrountes — invalidating, annulling, rendering void; a legal term for cancelling a contract). 'Word of God' versus 'your tradition' — the contrast is absolute. 'Many such like things' — the Corban example is just one illustration; the problem is systemic. Human tradition has annulled divine revelation.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had called all the people unto him, he said unto them, Hearken unto me every one of you, and understand:
verses.original_text: καὶ προσκαλεσάμενος πάντα τὸν ὄχλον ἔλεγεν αὐτοῖς ἀκούσατέ μου πάντες καὶ σύνετε
verses.transliteration: kai proskalesamenos panta ton ochlon elegen autois akousate mou pantes kai synete
verses.study_note: 'Called all the people' — Jesus moves from the private dispute with Pharisees to public teaching. 'Hearken' (akousate — listen, hear). 'Understand' (synete — comprehend, put together mentally). The double imperative signals the importance of what follows: Jesus is about to overturn centuries of purity legislation.

verses.kjv_text: There is nothing from without a man, that entering into him can defile him: but the things which come out of him, those are the things that defile the man.
verses.original_text: οὐδέν ἐστιν ἔξωθεν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου εἰσπορευόμενον εἰς αὐτὸν ὃ δύναται αὐτὸν κοινῶσαι ἀλλὰ τὰ ἐκ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐκπορευόμενά ἐστιν τὰ κοινοῦντα τὸν ἄνθρωπον
verses.transliteration: ouden estin exōthen tou anthrōpou eisporeuomenon eis auton ho dynatai auton koinōsai alla ta ek tou anthrōpou ekporeuomena estin ta koinounta ton anthrōpon
verses.study_note: 'Nothing from without' (ouden exōthen — nothing external). 'Defile' (koinōsai — make common, render unclean). 'Things which come out' (ta ekporeuomena — what proceeds from). This is one of the most revolutionary statements in the Gospels: defilement is relocated from the external to the internal, from the ritual to the moral. Jesus overturns the entire purity system with a single sentence.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is a theological earthquake. By declaring that nothing external can defile a person, Jesus challenges the entire Levitical food law system and the oral tradition built upon it. The implications were so radical that even the disciples needed private explanation (v.17). This teaching becomes the theological basis for Peter's vision in Acts 10:9-16 and Paul's declaration that 'there is nothing unclean of itself' (Romans 14:14). Defilement is a matter of the heart, not the diet.

verses.kjv_text: If any man have ears to hear, let him hear.
verses.original_text: εἴ τις ἔχει ὦτα ἀκούειν ἀκουέτω
verses.transliteration: ei tis echei ōta akouein akouetō
verses.study_note: A formulaic call to spiritual perception, used repeatedly by Jesus (cf. 4:9, 23). The implied warning: this teaching is so counter-cultural that many will refuse to accept it. Having physical ears is not enough — one must have spiritual ears willing to receive revolutionary truth.

verses.kjv_text: And when he was entered into the house from the people, his disciples asked him concerning the parable.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὅτε εἰσῆλθεν εἰς οἶκον ἀπὸ τοῦ ὄχλου ἐπηρώτων αὐτὸν οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ τὴν παραβολήν
verses.transliteration: kai hote eisēlthen eis oikon apo tou ochlou epērōtōn auton hoi mathētai autou tēn parabolēn
verses.study_note: 'The parable' (tēn parabolēn — the comparison, the figurative saying). The disciples need private explanation, a recurring pattern in Mark (cf. 4:10, 34). The public-private teaching dynamic reveals the progressive nature of discipleship: public proclamation followed by private clarification.

verses.kjv_text: And he saith unto them, Are ye so without understanding also? Do ye not perceive, that whatsoever thing from without entereth into the man, it cannot defile him;
verses.original_text: καὶ λέγει αὐτοῖς οὕτως καὶ ὑμεῖς ἀσύνετοί ἐστε οὐ νοεῖτε ὅτι πᾶν τὸ ἔξωθεν εἰσπορευόμενον εἰς τὸν ἄνθρωπον οὐ δύναται αὐτὸν κοινῶσαι
verses.transliteration: kai legei autois houtōs kai hymeis asynetoi este ou noeite hoti pan to exōthen eisporeuomenon eis ton anthrōpon ou dynatai auton koinōsai
verses.study_note: 'Without understanding' (asynetoi — lacking comprehension). 'Do ye not perceive' (ou noeite — do you not grasp?). Jesus is surprised at their slowness: even the disciples, who have been given 'the mystery of the kingdom' (4:11), struggle with this teaching. The abolition of the clean/unclean food distinction was deeply counter-intuitive for Jews.

verses.kjv_text: Because it entereth not into his heart, but into the belly, and goeth out into the draught, purging all meats?
verses.original_text: ὅτι οὐκ εἰσπορεύεται αὐτοῦ εἰς τὴν καρδίαν ἀλλ᾿ εἰς τὴν κοιλίαν καὶ εἰς τὸν ἀφεδρῶνα ἐκπορεύεται καθαρίζον πάντα τὰ βρώματα
verses.transliteration: hoti ouk eisporeuetai autou eis tēn kardian all eis tēn koilian kai eis ton aphedrōna ekporeuetai katharizon panta ta brōmata
verses.study_note: 'Heart' (kardian — the seat of moral and spiritual life). 'Belly' (koilian — stomach). 'Draught' (aphedrōna — latrine, toilet). 'Purging all meats' (katharizon panta ta brōmata — cleansing all foods). The parenthetical 'purging all meats' is Mark's editorial comment (or Jesus' own declaration): the entire food law system is declared obsolete. Food goes through the digestive system, not through the moral centre of a person.
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase 'purging all meats' (katharizon panta ta brōmata) is one of the most consequential parenthetical comments in Scripture. Whether this is Mark's editorial gloss or Jesus' own words, it declares all foods clean. This became the basis for the early church's abandonment of Jewish dietary laws (Acts 10:15; 15:28-29; Romans 14:14, 20; 1 Timothy 4:3-5). Jesus here does what no rabbi would dare: he overturns Levitical food laws by his own authority — an implicit claim to divine authority over the Torah itself.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, That which cometh out of the man, that defileth the man.
verses.original_text: ἔλεγεν δὲ ὅτι τὸ ἐκ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐκπορευόμενον ἐκεῖνο κοινοῖ τὸν ἄνθρωπον
verses.transliteration: elegen de hoti to ek tou anthrōpou ekporeuomenon ekeino koinoi ton anthrōpon
verses.study_note: Jesus restates the principle: defilement is an inside-out problem, not an outside-in problem. What proceeds from within — from the heart — is what makes a person unclean before God. This relocates the entire purity discussion from the ceremonial to the moral sphere.

verses.kjv_text: For from within, out of the heart of men, proceed evil thoughts, adulteries, fornications, murders,
verses.original_text: ἔσωθεν γὰρ ἐκ τῆς καρδίας τῶν ἀνθρώπων οἱ διαλογισμοὶ οἱ κακοὶ ἐκπορεύονται μοιχεῖαι πορνεῖαι φόνοι
verses.transliteration: esōthen gar ek tēs kardias tōn anthrōpōn hoi dialogismoi hoi kakoi ekporeuontai moicheiai porneiai phonoi
verses.study_note: 'From within' (esōthen — from inside). 'Heart' (kardias — the centre of human personality, will, and moral life). 'Evil thoughts' (dialogismoi kakoi — wicked reasonings). Then six plural nouns naming specific sins: adulteries (moicheiai), fornications (porneiai), murders (phonoi). The catalogue begins with sins of thought, then moves to sexual sins and violence. The heart is the factory of sin.
verses.doctrinal_note: This vice catalogue (vv.21-22) is one of Jesus' clearest statements on human depravity. Sin is not primarily a matter of environment, upbringing, or external contamination — it originates in the human heart. This aligns with Jeremiah 17:9 ('The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked') and anticipates Paul's teaching on the flesh (Galatians 5:19-21). The doctrine of original sin finds support here: the heart itself is the source of all evil.

verses.kjv_text: Thefts, covetousness, wickedness, deceit, lasciviousness, an evil eye, blasphemy, pride, foolishness:
verses.original_text: κλοπαί πλεονεξίαι πονηρίαι δόλος ἀσέλγεια ὀφθαλμὸς πονηρός βλασφημία ὑπερηφανία ἀφροσύνη
verses.transliteration: klopai pleonexiai ponēriai dolos aselgeia ophthalmos ponēros blasphēmia hyperēphania aphrosynē
verses.study_note: The list continues with seven more sins, now singular in Greek (indicating character traits rather than acts): thefts (klopai), covetousness (pleonexiai — greediness, wanting more), wickedness (ponēriai — active malice), deceit (dolos — cunning, treachery), lasciviousness (aselgeia — sensuality, shamelessness), an evil eye (ophthalmos ponēros — envy, stinginess), blasphemy (blasphēmia — slander against God or man), pride (hyperēphania — arrogance, setting oneself above others), foolishness (aphrosynē — moral senselessness, not intellectual deficiency).

verses.kjv_text: All these evil things come from within, and defile the man.
verses.original_text: πάντα ταῦτα τὰ πονηρὰ ἔσωθεν ἐκπορεύεται καὶ κοινοῖ τὸν ἄνθρωπον
verses.transliteration: panta tauta ta ponēra esōthen ekporeuetai kai koinoi ton anthrōpon
verses.study_note: 'All these' — comprehensive: every sin on the list. 'From within' (esōthen — from inside). 'Defile' (koinoi — make unclean). The conclusion is devastating: the human heart is the universal source of defilement. No amount of external washing can cleanse internal corruption. The only remedy is a new heart (Ezekiel 36:26).

verses.kjv_text: And from thence he arose, and went into the borders of Tyre and Sidon, and entered into an house, and would have no man know it: but he could not be hid.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐκεῖθεν ἀναστὰς ἀπῆλθεν εἰς τὰ μεθόρια Τύρου καὶ Σιδῶνος καὶ εἰσελθὼν εἰς οἰκίαν οὐδένα ἤθελεν γνῶναι καὶ οὐκ ἠδυνήθη λαθεῖν
verses.transliteration: kai ekeithen anastas apēlthen eis ta methoria Tyrou kai Sidōnos kai eiselthōn eis oikian oudena ēthelen gnōnai kai ouk ēdynēthē lathein
verses.study_note: 'Borders of Tyre and Sidon' (methoria — frontier regions). Jesus withdraws into Gentile territory — a dramatic geographical shift with theological significance. After declaring all foods clean (v.19), he crosses into unclean territory. 'Could not be hid' — his reputation precedes him even among pagans. The physical journey mirrors the theological movement: from Israel to the nations.

verses.kjv_text: For a certain woman, whose young daughter had an unclean spirit, heard of him, and came and fell at his feet:
verses.original_text: ἀκούσασα γὰρ γυνὴ περὶ αὐτοῦ ἧς εἶχεν τὸ θυγάτριον αὐτῆς πνεῦμα ἀκάθαρτον ἐλθοῦσα προσέπεσεν πρὸς τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: akousasa gar gynē peri autou hēs eichen to thygatrion autēs pneuma akatharton elthousa prosepesen pros tous podas autou
verses.study_note: 'Young daughter' (thygatrion — diminutive: little daughter, expressing tenderness). 'Unclean spirit' (pneuma akatharton). 'Fell at his feet' (prosepesen — prostrated herself). A desperate Gentile mother with a demonized child — she overcomes every barrier (gender, ethnicity, religion) to reach Jesus. Her posture of humility contrasts with the Pharisees' posture of superiority.

verses.kjv_text: The woman was a Greek, a Syrophenician by nation; and she besought him that he would cast forth the devil out of her daughter.
verses.original_text: ἡ δὲ γυνὴ ἦν Ἑλληνίς Συροφοινίκισσα τῷ γένει καὶ ἠρώτα αὐτὸν ἵνα τὸ δαιμόνιον ἐκβάλῃ ἐκ τῆς θυγατρὸς αὐτῆς
verses.transliteration: hē de gynē ēn Hellēnis Syrophoinikissa tō genei kai ērōta auton hina to daimonion ekbalē ek tēs thygatros autēs
verses.study_note: 'Greek' (Hellēnis — a Gentile, pagan; culturally and religiously non-Jewish). 'Syrophoenician' (Syrophoinikissa — from Syrian Phoenicia, distinguishing her from Libyan Phoenicians in North Africa). Mark layers her identity to emphasise her utter outsider status: she is female, Greek-speaking, Gentile, and from a region historically hostile to Israel.

verses.kjv_text: But Jesus said unto her, Let the children first be filled: for it is not meet to take the children's bread, and to cast it unto the dogs.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν αὐτῇ ἄφες πρῶτον χορτασθῆναι τὰ τέκνα οὐ γάρ ἐστιν καλὸν λαβεῖν τὸν ἄρτον τῶν τέκνων καὶ τοῖς κυναρίοις βαλεῖν
verses.transliteration: ho de Iēsous eipen autē aphes prōton chortasthēnai ta tekna ou gar estin kalon labein ton arton tōn teknōn kai tois kynariois balein
verses.study_note: 'Let the children first be filled' (prōton chortasthēnai — first be satisfied). 'Children' (tekna — Israel). 'Dogs' (kynariois — diminutive: little dogs, household pets, not wild scavengers). 'First' is crucial — it implies a sequence, not an exclusion. Israel's priority does not mean Gentile exclusion. Jesus tests her faith, not denies it. The diminutive 'little dogs' softens the image — these are pets under the table, not strays in the street.
verses.doctrinal_note: This exchange is often misunderstood as Jesus being rude or nationalistic. Several features indicate a test of faith, not rejection: (1) 'first' (prōton) implies a 'then' — Gentiles will eat, just not yet; (2) kynariois is the diminutive 'puppies/pets,' not the harsh 'dogs' (kynes) used elsewhere; (3) Jesus has already been in Gentile territory performing miracles; (4) the outcome proves he was drawing out her faith for display. Jesus operates within salvation-historical priority (to the Jew first, Romans 1:16) while opening the door to universal grace.

verses.kjv_text: And she answered and said unto him, Yes, Lord: yet the dogs under the table eat of the children's crumbs.
verses.original_text: ἡ δὲ ἀπεκρίθη καὶ λέγει αὐτῷ ναί κύριε καὶ γὰρ τὰ κυνάρια ὑποκάτω τῆς τραπέζης ἐσθίουσιν ἀπὸ τῶν ψιχίων τῶν παιδίων
verses.transliteration: hē de apekrithē kai legei autō nai kyrie kai gar ta kynaria hypokatō tēs trapezēs esthiousin apo tōn psichiōn tōn paidiōn
verses.study_note: 'Yes, Lord' (nai kyrie — she accepts the premise, does not argue the priority of Israel). 'Yet' (kai gar — and indeed, even so). 'Under the table' (hypokatō tēs trapezēs — she accepts her position). 'Crumbs' (psichiōn — fragments, morsels). Her answer is a masterpiece of faith: she does not challenge Jesus' framework but works within it. She does not demand equality but requests overflow. If Israel's table is so rich, even the crumbs are enough to deliver her daughter.
verses.doctrinal_note: This woman's response is one of the greatest expressions of faith in the Gospels. She does not dispute the priority of Israel ('Yes, Lord'), nor does she demand equal status. She simply recognises that God's grace is so abundant that even its overflow — the crumbs — is sufficient for her need. This is the logic of grace: God's provision is so vast that what falls from Israel's table can heal the nations. Her faith sees abundance where others see scarcity.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto her, For this saying go thy way; the devil is gone out of thy daughter.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῇ διὰ τοῦτον τὸν λόγον ὕπαγε ἐξελήλυθεν τὸ δαιμόνιον ἐκ τῆς θυγατρός σου
verses.transliteration: kai eipen autē dia touton ton logon hypage exelēlythen to daimonion ek tēs thygatros sou
verses.study_note: 'For this saying' (dia touton ton logon — because of this word). Jesus rewards her faith expressed in her reply. The healing is accomplished at a distance — no touch, no command to the demon, no physical presence required. 'The devil is gone out' (exelēlythen — perfect tense: has gone out and remains out). This is the only miracle in Mark performed solely on the basis of the supplicant's verbal response.

verses.kjv_text: And when she was come to her house, she found the devil gone out, and her daughter laid upon the bed.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπελθοῦσα εἰς τὸν οἶκον αὐτῆς εὗρεν τὸ παιδίον βεβλημένον ἐπὶ τὴν κλίνην καὶ τὸ δαιμόνιον ἐξεληλυθός
verses.transliteration: kai apelthousa eis ton oikon autēs heuren to paidion beblēmenon epi tēn klinēn kai to daimonion exelēlythos
verses.study_note: 'Laid upon the bed' (beblēmenon epi tēn klinēn — lying on the bed; peaceful rest replacing demonic torment). 'The devil gone out' (daimonion exelēlythos — perfect participle: the demon had departed). The woman returns home to find the word of Jesus already accomplished. No fanfare, no spectacle — just a child at rest and a demon gone.

verses.kjv_text: And again, departing from the coasts of Tyre and Sidon, he came unto the sea of Galilee, through the midst of the coasts of Decapolis.
verses.original_text: καὶ πάλιν ἐξελθὼν ἐκ τῶν ὁρίων Τύρου καὶ Σιδῶνος ἦλθεν πρὸς τὴν θάλασσαν τῆς Γαλιλαίας ἀνὰ μέσον τῶν ὁρίων Δεκαπόλεως
verses.transliteration: kai palin exelthōn ek tōn horiōn Tyrou kai Sidōnos ēlthen pros tēn thalassan tēs Galilaias ana meson tōn horiōn Dekapoleōs
verses.study_note: 'Decapolis' (Dekapoleōs — a league of ten Greco-Roman cities, predominantly Gentile). Jesus takes a circuitous route through Gentile territory, moving from Phoenicia south through the Decapolis region east of the Jordan. This extended journey through pagan lands reinforces the theme: the gospel is breaking beyond Israel's borders.

verses.kjv_text: And they bring unto him one that was deaf, and had an impediment in his speech; and they beseech him to put his hand upon him.
verses.original_text: καὶ φέρουσιν αὐτῷ κωφὸν μογιλάλον καὶ παρακαλοῦσιν αὐτὸν ἵνα ἐπιθῇ αὐτῷ τὴν χεῖρα
verses.transliteration: kai pherousin autō kōphon mogilalon kai parakalousin auton hina epithē autō tēn cheira
verses.study_note: 'Deaf' (kōphon — deaf, or possibly deaf-mute). 'Impediment in his speech' (mogilalon — speaking with difficulty; used only here and in Isaiah 35:6 LXX, creating a deliberate echo of Isaiah's messianic prophecy). 'Put his hand upon him' — the friends request physical touch, expecting a method of healing. The rare word mogilalon signals to literate readers that messianic prophecy is being fulfilled.

verses.kjv_text: And he took him aside from the multitude, and put his fingers into his ears, and he spit, and touched his tongue;
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπολαβόμενος αὐτὸν ἀπὸ τοῦ ὄχλου κατ᾿ ἰδίαν ἔβαλεν τοὺς δακτύλους αὐτοῦ εἰς τὰ ὦτα αὐτοῦ καὶ πτύσας ἥψατο τῆς γλώσσης αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai apolabomenos auton apo tou ochlou kat idian ebalen tous daktylous autou eis ta ōta autou kai ptysas hēpsato tēs glōssēs autou
verses.study_note: 'Took him aside' (apolabomenos — withdrew him; private, personal attention). 'Fingers into his ears' — communicating through touch what cannot be communicated by sound to a deaf man. 'Spit, and touched his tongue' — saliva was widely believed to have healing properties in antiquity. Jesus uses physical gestures as sign language: I am going to heal your ears and your tongue. The method accommodates the man's condition.

verses.kjv_text: And looking up to heaven, he sighed, and saith unto him, Ephphatha, that is, Be opened.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀναβλέψας εἰς τὸν οὐρανὸν ἐστέναξεν καὶ λέγει αὐτῷ εφφαθα ὅ ἐστιν διανοίχθητι
verses.transliteration: kai anablepsas eis ton ouranon estenaxen kai legei autō ephphatha ho estin dianoichthēti
verses.study_note: 'Looking up to heaven' (anablepsas eis ton ouranon — indicating the source of healing power). 'Sighed' (estenaxen — groaned deeply; an expression of profound emotion, compassion, and perhaps grief over the effects of sin and disease). 'Ephphatha' — Aramaic, preserved in the original language for its power and memorability. 'Be opened' (dianoichthēti — be thoroughly opened; the passive voice indicates divine action). Mark translates the Aramaic for his Gentile readers.

verses.kjv_text: And straightway his ears were opened, and the string of his tongue was loosed, and he spake plain.
verses.original_text: καὶ εὐθέως διηνοίχθησαν αὐτοῦ αἱ ἀκοαί καὶ ἐλύθη ὁ δεσμὸς τῆς γλώσσης αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐλάλει ὀρθῶς
verses.transliteration: kai eutheōs diēnoichthēsan autou hai akoai kai elythē ho desmos tēs glōssēs autou kai elalei orthōs
verses.study_note: 'Straightway' (eutheōs — immediately; Mark's characteristic word). 'Ears were opened' (diēnoichthēsan — thoroughly opened). 'String of his tongue was loosed' (elythē ho desmos — the bond was released; suggesting demonic or physical binding). 'Spake plain' (elalei orthōs — spoke correctly, properly). The healing is instantaneous and complete — no gradual recovery, no speech therapy needed.

verses.kjv_text: And he charged them that they should tell no man: but the more he charged them, so much the more a great deal they published it;
verses.original_text: καὶ διεστείλατο αὐτοῖς ἵνα μηδενὶ εἴπωσιν ὅσον δὲ αὐτὸς αὐτοῖς διεστέλλετο μᾶλλον περισσότερον ἐκήρυσσον
verses.transliteration: kai diesteilato autois hina mēdeni eipōsin hoson de autos autois diestelleto mallon perissoteron ekērysson
verses.study_note: 'Charged them' (diesteilato — commanded, ordered firmly). 'The more he charged... so much the more' — a pattern of inverse obedience unique to Mark. 'Published it' (ekērysson — proclaimed, heralded). The messianic secret motif: Jesus suppresses publicity, but the evidence is too compelling. Ironically, those he commands to speak (the Gerasene demoniac, 5:19) obey, while those he commands to silence cannot contain themselves.

verses.kjv_text: And were beyond measure astonished, saying, He hath done all things well: he maketh both the deaf to hear, and the dumb to speak.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὑπερπερισσῶς ἐξεπλήσσοντο λέγοντες καλῶς πάντα πεποίηκεν καὶ τοὺς κωφοὺς ποιεῖ ἀκούειν καὶ τοὺς ἀλάλους λαλεῖν
verses.transliteration: kai hyperperissōs exeplēssonto legontes kalōs panta pepoiēken kai tous kōphous poiei akouein kai tous alalous lalein
verses.study_note: 'Beyond measure astonished' (hyperperissōs exeplēssonto — super-abundantly amazed; hyperperissōs is a rare double-compound found only here in the NT). 'He hath done all things well' (kalōs panta pepoiēken — echoing Genesis 1:31 LXX: God saw all that he had made, and it was very good). 'The deaf to hear and the dumb to speak' — echoing Isaiah 35:5-6. The crowd unwittingly identifies Jesus as both Creator and Messiah through their exclamation.
verses.doctrinal_note: The crowd's declaration 'He hath done all things well' carries two profound echoes: (1) Genesis 1:31 — God's creation verdict. Jesus is performing a new creation, restoring what sin has broken. (2) Isaiah 35:5-6 — the messianic age when 'the ears of the deaf shall be unstopped' and 'the tongue of the dumb sing.' This Gentile crowd, perhaps unknowingly, confesses Jesus as both Creator and the Promised One. The healing of deaf ears and mute tongues among the nations fulfils the very prophecy of the gospel's universal reach.

2.meaning: Common, defiled, ritually unclean — from koinoō (to make common). In secular Greek, koinos simply means 'shared' or 'communal' (cf. Acts 2:44, 'had all things common'). In Jewish religious usage, it acquired the specialised meaning of 'profane' or 'ritually impure' — something that has not been properly consecrated or has been contaminated by contact with the unclean. Jesus redefines the concept: true defilement is moral, not ceremonial.

6.meaning: Hypocrite, pretender, actor — from hypokrinomai (to answer from under, to play a part on stage). In classical Greek, the word referred to stage actors who wore masks to portray characters. Jesus applies it to religious leaders whose outward piety masks inward emptiness. The lips honour God while the heart is far away. Hypocrisy is the most dangerous form of unbelief because it mimics faith.

11.meaning: Corban, an offering, a gift dedicated to God — a Hebrew/Aramaic loanword (from qorban, 'that which is brought near' to the altar). In rabbinic practice, a person could declare property 'Corban' (devoted to God), thereby making it unavailable for any other use — including supporting elderly parents. The property might never actually reach the temple; the declaration simply shielded it. Jesus exposes this as using religious vocabulary to evade moral duty.

15.meaning: To make common, defile, render unclean — the verb form of koinos. Jesus uses this word to overturn the entire purity system: nothing entering from outside can koinōsai (defile) a person. Defilement is relocated from the external (food, unwashed hands) to the internal (the heart's moral condition). This single verb, in Jesus' usage, dismantles centuries of ceremonial legislation and anticipates the Gentile mission.

19.meaning: To cleanse, purify, make clean — from katharos (clean, pure). The participle katharizon ('purging/cleansing') in this verse is either Mark's editorial comment or Jesus' own declaration. Either way, it pronounces all foods clean (katharizon panta ta brōmata). This word becomes central to the Acts 10 vision ('What God hath cleansed, call not thou common') and Paul's food ethics in Romans 14.

22.meaning: Lasciviousness, sensuality, wanton excess — denoting open, shameless indulgence without restraint or regard for decency. Unlike hidden sins, aselgeia flaunts itself. It appears in Paul's vice lists (Galatians 5:19; Ephesians 4:19; 2 Peter 2:2, 7). In Jesus' catalogue of heart-sins, it represents the most brazen form of moral corruption — sin that has lost all sense of shame.

27.meaning: Little dog, household pet, puppy — diminutive of kyōn (dog). In Jewish usage, 'dog' (kyōn) was a term of contempt for Gentiles (cf. Philippians 3:2; Revelation 22:15). But Jesus uses the diminutive kynarion — not wild scavengers but small dogs kept as household pets, allowed under the family table. The diminutive softens the image significantly and opens the door: pets belong in the household, even if they eat after the children.

34.meaning: Be opened — an Aramaic imperative (from the root pathach, to open). One of the few Aramaic words preserved in Mark's Greek text (cf. 5:41, Talitha koum; 14:36, Abba; 15:34, Eloi). Mark preserves the original language for its vividness and power, then translates for his Gentile audience. The word becomes a command not just to deaf ears but to all that is closed — hearts, minds, borders, traditions.

meta.overview: Mark 8 is the structural centre and dramatic turning point of the entire Gospel. The chapter opens with the feeding of the 4,000 in Gentile territory (vv.1-9) — a deliberate parallel to the feeding of the 5,000 (6:30-44), now extending Jesus' provision to the nations. The Pharisees then demand a sign from heaven, which Jesus refuses with 'a deep sigh' (vv.10-13). On the boat, Jesus warns the disciples about 'the leaven of the Pharisees and Herod,' but they misunderstand, worrying about literal bread (vv.14-21). Jesus rebukes them: 'Having eyes, see ye not? and having ears, hear ye not?' — echoing the very blindness he denounced in chapter 4. At Bethsaida, Jesus heals a blind man in two stages — first partial sight ('I see men as trees, walking'), then full clarity (vv.22-26). This unique two-stage miracle becomes a living parable of the disciples' own spiritual sight, which is partial but about to become clear. Then comes the watershed moment: at Caesarea Philippi, Jesus asks, 'Whom do men say that I am?' and then the piercing personal question, 'But whom say ye that I am?' Peter answers, 'Thou art the Christ' (vv.27-30). This confession divides the Gospel in half. Immediately Jesus begins to teach 'that the Son of man must suffer many things, and be rejected... and be killed, and after three days rise again' (v.31). Peter rebukes Jesus and receives the most severe rebuke in the Gospels: 'Get thee behind me, Satan' (v.33). The chapter concludes with Jesus calling the crowd and disciples to radical discipleship: 'Whosoever will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me' (v.34). The paradox of the kingdom: 'whosoever will save his life shall lose it; but whosoever shall lose his life for my sake and the gospel's, the same shall save it' (v.35). 'What shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?' (v.36).
meta.theme: The Turning Point — From Miracles to the Cross, From Partial Sight to Full Confession
meta.keyWordMeaning: Christ, the Anointed One — from chriō (to anoint with oil). The Greek translation of Hebrew Mashiach (Messiah). Peter's confession 'Thou art the Christ' (sy ei ho Christos) is the climactic moment of the first half of Mark. But immediately Jesus redefines the Messiah: not a political conqueror but a suffering servant who must be rejected, killed, and raised. The title is correct; the popular understanding of it is wrong.
outline: Feeding the Four Thousand in Gentile Territory (vv.1-9): Jesus has compassion on a great multitude that has been with him three days with nothing to eat. Seven loaves and a few small fishes feed 4000 men with seven baskets of fragments remaining. The feeding of the Gentiles mirrors and extends the feeding of the 5000

The Leaven of the Pharisees and the Disciples' Blindness (vv.10-21): The Pharisees demand a sign from heaven and Jesus refuses. In the boat he warns of the leaven of the Pharisees and Herod but the disciples think he means literal bread. Jesus rebukes their hardened hearts — having eyes they do not see having ears they do not hear. Do ye not yet understand?

The Two-Stage Healing of the Blind Man at Bethsaida (vv.22-26): Jesus heals a blind man in two stages — first partial sight (I see men as trees walking) then complete clarity. This unique graduated miracle is a living parable: the disciples can partially see who Jesus is but full understanding will come only through the cross

Peter's Confession and the First Passion Prediction (vv.27-33): At Caesarea Philippi Peter confesses Thou art the Christ. Jesus immediately teaches that the Son of man must suffer be rejected be killed and rise after three days. Peter rebukes Jesus and is rebuked as Satan — setting his mind on the things of men not God

The Cost of Discipleship (vv.34-38): Jesus calls the crowd and disciples to deny themselves take up the cross and follow him. Whoever saves his life will lose it whoever loses his life for Jesus and the gospel will save it. What shall it profit a man to gain the whole world and lose his own soul

verses.kjv_text: In those days the multitude being very great, and having nothing to eat, Jesus called his disciples unto him, and saith unto them,
verses.original_text: ἐν ἐκείναις ταῖς ἡμέραις πάλιν πολλοῦ ὄχλου ὄντος καὶ μὴ ἐχόντων τί φάγωσιν προσκαλεσάμενος τοὺς μαθητὰς αὐτοῦ λέγει αὐτοῖς
verses.transliteration: en ekeinais tais hēmerais palin pollou ochlou ontos kai mē echontōn ti phagōsin proskalesamenos tous mathētas autou legei autois
verses.study_note: 'In those days' — the context is still Gentile territory (Decapolis, cf. 7:31). 'The multitude being very great' — a second large crowd follows Jesus, this time predominantly Gentile. 'Having nothing to eat' — their need is genuine, their devotion evident in remaining with Jesus despite hunger. Jesus initiates the solution; the crowd does not ask.

verses.kjv_text: I have compassion on the multitude, because they have now been with me three days, and have nothing to eat:
verses.original_text: σπλαγχνίζομαι ἐπὶ τὸν ὄχλον ὅτι ἤδη ἡμέραι τρεῖς προσμένουσίν μοι καὶ οὐκ ἔχουσιν τί φάγωσιν
verses.transliteration: splanchnizomai epi ton ochlon hoti ēdē hēmerai treis prosmenousin moi kai ouk echousin ti phagōsin
verses.study_note: 'Compassion' (splanchnizomai — to be moved in the bowels, deeply stirred with compassion; the strongest Greek word for pity). 'Three days' — a long time without food, showing the crowd's extraordinary dedication. Jesus does not only teach spiritual truth; he cares about physical needs. The same compassion that fed the 5,000 Jews now feeds Gentiles — God's provision knows no ethnic boundary.

verses.kjv_text: And if I send them away fasting to their own houses, they will faint by the way: for divers of them came from far.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐὰν ἀπολύσω αὐτοὺς νήστεις εἰς οἶκον αὐτῶν ἐκλυθήσονται ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ τινὲς γὰρ αὐτῶν μακρόθεν ἥκασιν
verses.transliteration: kai ean apolysō autous nēsteis eis oikon autōneklythēsontai en tē hodō tines gar autōn makrothen hēkasin
verses.study_note: 'Faint' (eklythēsontai — collapse, give out from exhaustion). 'From far' (makrothen — from a distance). Jesus shows practical concern for their physical safety. This detail — some came from far — may hint at the Gentile mission: those who were 'far off' (Ephesians 2:13) are being brought near.

verses.kjv_text: And his disciples answered him, From whence can a man satisfy these men with bread here in the wilderness?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπεκρίθησαν αὐτῷ οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ ὅτι πόθεν τούτους δυνήσεταί τις ὧδε χορτάσαι ἄρτων ἐπ᾿ ἐρημίας
verses.transliteration: kai apekrithēsan autō hoi mathētai autou hoti pothen toutous dynēsetai tis hōde chortasai artōn ep erēmias
verses.study_note: The disciples' question is astonishing: they have already witnessed the feeding of the 5,000 (6:35-44), yet they ask 'From whence?' (pothen — from what source?). 'In the wilderness' (ep erēmias — in a desolate place). Their amnesia illustrates a pattern in Mark: spiritual lessons must be learned repeatedly. Past miracles do not automatically produce present faith.

verses.kjv_text: And he asked them, How many loaves have ye? And they said, Seven.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπηρώτα αὐτούς πόσους ἔχετε ἄρτους οἱ δὲ εἶπον ἑπτά
verses.transliteration: kai epērōta autous posous echete artous hoi de eipon hepta
verses.study_note: 'Seven' (hepta) — in Jewish symbolism, seven represents completeness and perfection (cf. the seven days of creation). While five loaves fed 5,000 Jews (representing the Torah/Pentateuch), seven loaves feed the Gentile crowd — perhaps symbolising the fullness of God's provision for all nations.

verses.kjv_text: And he commanded the people to sit down on the ground: and he took the seven loaves, and gave thanks, and brake, and gave to his disciples to set before them; and they did set them before the people.
verses.original_text: καὶ παρήγγειλεν τῷ ὄχλῳ ἀναπεσεῖν ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς καὶ λαβὼν τοὺς ἑπτὰ ἄρτους εὐχαριστήσας ἔκλασεν καὶ ἐδίδου τοῖς μαθηταῖς αὐτοῦ ἵνα παραθῶσιν καὶ παρέθηκαν τῷ ὄχλῳ
verses.transliteration: kai parēngeilen tō ochlō anapesein epi tēs gēs kai labōn tous hepta artous eucharistēsas eklasen kai edidou tois mathētais autou hina parathōsin kai parethēkan tō ochlō
verses.study_note: 'Gave thanks' (eucharistēsas — from eucharistia, thanksgiving; the word from which 'Eucharist' derives). The pattern mirrors the Last Supper: took, gave thanks, broke, gave. The disciples serve as intermediaries between Jesus and the crowd — a pattern for ministry in every age.

verses.kjv_text: And they had a few small fishes: and he blessed, and commanded to set them also before them.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶχον ἰχθύδια ὀλίγα καὶ εὐλογήσας αὐτὰ εἶπεν καὶ ταῦτα παρατιθέναι
verses.transliteration: kai eichon ichthydia oliga kai eulogēsas auta eipen kai tauta paratithenai
verses.study_note: 'Small fishes' (ichthydia — diminutive: little fish, tiny fish). 'Blessed' (eulogēsas — spoke well of, praised God for). The separate blessing of the fish may indicate they were brought to Jesus after the bread was distributed. The small size and few number emphasise the miracle: the provision is entirely disproportionate to the supply.

verses.kjv_text: So they did eat, and were filled: and they took up of the broken meat that was left seven baskets full.
verses.original_text: ἔφαγον δὲ καὶ ἐχορτάσθησαν καὶ ἦραν περισσεύματα κλασμάτων ἑπτὰ σπυρίδας
verses.transliteration: ephagon de kai echortasthēsan kai ēran perisseumata klasmatōn hepta spyridas
verses.study_note: 'Were filled' (echortasthēsan — satisfied, fed to the full). 'Seven baskets' (hepta spyridas — large woven baskets, different from the kophinos baskets used in the feeding of the 5,000). Spyris is a large hamper-basket (cf. Acts 9:25, Paul lowered in a spyris). Seven baskets of surplus — God's abundance exceeds human need.

verses.kjv_text: And they that had eaten were about four thousand: and he sent them away.
verses.original_text: ἦσαν δὲ ὡς τετρακισχίλιοι καὶ ἀπέλυσεν αὐτούς
verses.transliteration: ēsan de hōs tetrakischilioi kai apelysen autous
verses.study_note: Four thousand (tetrakischilioi) — men alone; with women and children, the number would be considerably larger. The two feeding miracles together (5,000 + 4,000) demonstrate Jesus as the bread of life for all peoples — Jew and Gentile alike.

verses.kjv_text: And straightway he entered into a ship with his disciples, and came into the parts of Dalmanutha.
verses.original_text: καὶ εὐθέως ἐμβὰς εἰς τὸ πλοῖον μετὰ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ ἦλθεν εἰς τὰ μέρη Δαλμανουθά
verses.transliteration: kai eutheōs embas eis to ploion meta tōn mathētōn autou ēlthen eis ta merē Dalmanoutha
verses.study_note: 'Dalmanutha' — a location on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee, mentioned only here in Scripture. Matthew's parallel (15:39) says 'Magdala.' Jesus returns to Jewish territory after the Gentile ministry, setting up the Pharisaic confrontation that follows.

verses.kjv_text: And the Pharisees came forth, and began to question with him, seeking of him a sign from heaven, tempting him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐξῆλθον οἱ Φαρισαῖοι καὶ ἤρξαντο συζητεῖν αὐτῷ ζητοῦντες παρ᾿ αὐτοῦ σημεῖον ἀπὸ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ πειράζοντες αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: kai exēlthon hoi Pharisaioi kai ērxanto syzētein autō zētountes par autou sēmeion apo tou ouranou peirazontes auton
verses.study_note: 'Sign from heaven' (sēmeion apo tou ouranou — a cosmic portent, an unmistakable divine endorsement). 'Tempting' (peirazontes — testing, putting to the test; the same word used of Satan's temptation in 1:13). The Pharisees have seen earthly miracles and remain unconvinced; now they demand a heavenly spectacle. Their request is not genuine inquiry but hostile testing.

verses.kjv_text: And he sighed deeply in his spirit, and saith, Why doth this generation seek after a sign? verily I say unto you, There shall no sign be given unto this generation.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀναστενάξας τῷ πνεύματι αὐτοῦ λέγει τί ἡ γενεὰ αὕτη ζητεῖ σημεῖον ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν εἰ δοθήσεται τῇ γενεᾷ ταύτῃ σημεῖον
verses.transliteration: kai anastenaxas tō pneumati autou legei ti hē genea hautē zētei sēmeion amēn legō hymin ei dothēsetai tē genea tautē sēmeion
